retrieved_docs.title: Chloroprocaine -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Local anesthetics that exhibit higher degrees of plasma protein binding generally have a longer duration of action.  This is because protein binding in plasma can be used as a surrogate for local anesthetic affinity for protein binding in the sodium channel. [14] Highly protein-bound molecules such as bupivacaine exhibit a longer duration of action than lesser protein-bound molecules such as lidocaine. Chloroprocaine has the lowest protein binding of all clinically used local anesthetics and is amongst the shortest in duration of action. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Topical, Local, and Regional Anesthesia and Anesthetics -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Local anesthetics are used to anesthetize the skin, subcutaneous tissue, and peripheral nerves during invasive or surgical procedures. The duration of action of local anesthetics can range from 30 minutes to 12 hours or more, depending on factors such as the location of the block (eg, areas with a high blood supply tend to have a shorter duration), the specific anesthetic used, and its formulation (eg, liposomal preparations may offer extended-release effects). Commonly used local anesthetics are classified into 2 main groups—amino amides and amino esters—each with distinct pharmacokinetic properties and clinical applications. Amino amides: Common agents in this class are listed below. Mepivacaine Lidocaine Etidocaine Bupivacaine Levobupivacaine Ropivacaine Amino esters: Common agents in this class are listed below. Procaine Cocaine Chloroprocaine Tetracaine Benzocaine

retrieved_docs.title: Tetracaine -- Indications -- Off-Label Uses
retrieved_docs.content: Topical tetracaine administered over 24 hours for corneal abrasions appears safe and doesn't delay healing. While pain scores were similar, patients reported that tetracaine was more effective than saline drops. [3] [4] Tetracaine has also been considered for strabismus surgeries. [5] The American College of Emergency Physicians (ACEP) recommends administering a commercial topical anesthetic (proparacaine/tetracaine/oxybuprocaine) for simple corneal abrasions; this medication should be administered with care. Tetracaine can be safely administered every 30 minutes as needed within the first 24 hours, with a maximum total dose of 2 mL. Any remaining solution must be discarded after 24 hours. [5] [6] The American Academy of Ophthalmology (AAO) doesn't endorse the routine prescription of topical anesthetics such as tetracaine for corneal abrasions, citing the known risks of corneal toxicity and permanent damage, including toxic keratopathy associated with unsupervised use. The AAO notes that studies referenced in emergency medicine guidelines lack ophthalmology follow-up and are insufficiently powered to detect adverse outcomes. [7] Lidocaine/tetracaine 7%/7% peel cream (L/T-pC) provides effective pain control during fractional CO 2 laser resurfacing for facial skin aging, with a 65% reduction in pain scores. This combination was well tolerated, with 80% of patients achieving adequate anesthesia (VAS ≤3), supporting its use as a reliable topical anesthetic in dermatologic laser procedures. [8] Tetracaine can also be applied topically for IV line insertion/venipuncture in children. [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Regional Anesthesia -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Ideal body weight is used for the calculation of the maximal allowable dose of local anesthetic. This is 3mg/kg for bupivacaine, levobupivacaine, and ropivacaine, 5 mg/kg for lidocaine without epinephrine, and 7 mg/kg for lidocaine with epinephrine for bolus dosing. Toxicities of different local anesthetic drugs are additive. If a maximal dose of one LA has already been used, a different LA should not be used. Infants less than 6 months require at least a 30% reduction in the maximal doses due to increased free fractions and decreased elimination. An epidural infusion dose of 0.4 to 0.5 mg/kg/hr is used for bupivacaine (0.25%), levobupivacaine (0.25%), and ropivacaine (0.2%); this is reduced to 0.25 mg/kg/hr for infants less than 6 months. Chloroprocaine (2%) is a rapid, short-acting ester LA used in infants at a dose of 8 to 10 mg/kg/hr. It is rapidly metabolized and eliminated by plasma esterases. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Chloroprocaine -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Chloroprocaine is an exception to this rule. Chloroprocaine has a pKa (8.7) greater than lidocaine, ropivacaine, bupivacaine, and mepivacaine, yet it can provide faster onset epidural anesthesia. [14] Chloroprocaine can provide fast onset anesthesia because it has a very low risk for systemic toxicity (it is rapidly metabolized by pseudocholinesterase) and can be administered in high doses, which creates a large diffusion gradient to drive quick onset.

retrieved_docs.title: Chloroprocaine -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Local anesthetics are not specific for peripheral nerves; nerves in the central nervous system and cardiac conduction system may also be blocked.  Because of this, severe symptoms involving the central nervous and cardiovascular system may result when large doses of local anesthetic are injected—a syndrome commonly referred to as local anesthetic systemic toxicity (LAST).

retrieved_docs.title: Tetracaine -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Absorption: Tetracaine has a pKa of 8.46 at room temperature (25 °C), which is moderate. The drug has a relatively quick onset of action, especially when administered intrathecally. Lipid solubility for tetracaine is high, with a relative value of 80, making it among the most potent local anesthetics. Distribution: The protein binding is moderate at 75%, allowing a relative duration of action of up to 200 minutes.

retrieved_docs.title: Chloroprocaine -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: The temperature should be assessed whenever clinically meaningful changes in body temperature are expected. Due to the short duration of procedures that involve chloroprocaine administration, temperature monitoring is usually not required while blocks are being performed.

retrieved_docs.title: Chloroprocaine -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Epidural chloroprocaine is commonly used in the obstetric setting to provide rapid onset anesthesia for emergent cesarean delivery when an epidural catheter is in place. Chloroprocaine can be rapidly administered in large doses to provide rapid onset anesthesia without risking maternal or fetal systemic toxicity. The half-life of chloroprocaine in maternal and fetal plasma is 11.2 seconds and 15.4 seconds, respectively. [4] Due to its rapid metabolism, toxic concentrations of chloroprocaine are difficult to achieve.

retrieved_docs.title: Local Anesthetic Drugs Used In Dentistry -- Summary / Explanation -- Mepivacaine
retrieved_docs.content: Mepivacaine is an essential amide local anesthetic in dentistry, known for its rapid onset and moderate duration of action. Similar to prilocaine, mepivacaine has less vasodilating activity than lidocaine. This property allows it to be used without a vasoconstrictor, allowing the administration of higher concentrations of plain 3% mepivacaine directly at the site. [13] As a result, it serves as a practical alternative to lidocaine or articaine combined with adrenaline.

retrieved_docs.title: Chloroprocaine -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the approved and off-label indications for chloroprocaine. Summarize the mechanism of action of chloroprocaine. Outline the necessary monitoring of chloroprocaine. Describe the importance of interprofessional team members to improve patient outcomes in patients receiving chloroprocaine for local anesthesia. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Local Anesthetics in Children -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: The actions of LAs are affected by the pH, the concentration of calcium, and the stimulation of the nerve. Increased dose, concentration, and volume of an LA can prolong block duration by as much as 50%. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Chloroprocaine -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Chloroprocaine is an ester class local anesthetic and is indicated for neuraxial anesthesia (caudal, epidural, and spinal) and peripheral nerve blocks and obstetric anesthesia (pudendal and paracervical blocks). Due to its short duration, it has been used in test doses to evaluate the function of peripheral nerve and epidural catheters. This activity reviews the mechanism of action, adverse event profile, toxicity, dosing, pharmacodynamics, and monitoring of chloroprocaine, pertinent for interprofessional team members where patient care requires local anesthesia that would be met using chloroprocaine.

retrieved_docs.title: Chloroprocaine -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: In general, the speed of onset for a local anesthetic is determined by its pKa (dissociation constant)—the pH at which half the drug is ionized (protonated) and half the drug is nonionized (uncharged). [13] Local anesthetics are stored in water-based solutions. Local anesthetics must be in ionized form to be stable in water-based solutions.  However, only the nonionized form of the local anesthetic can cross the nerve lipid membrane and block the sodium channel. All local anesthetics, including chloroprocaine, are weak bases (pKa > 7.0) with a pKa above physiologic pH (7.4). [14] Consequently, lower pKa is associated with a greater proportion of nonionized drugs, which typically equates to faster onset of action. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Local and Regional Anesthesia in Ophthalmology and Ocular Trauma -- Equipment -- Anesthetic Agents
retrieved_docs.content: The choice of local anesthetic agent is based on its pharmacologic properties and availability. The surgeon or anesthesiologist may choose to use a single agent alone or a combination of agents. The injected anesthetic agents most often used include lidocaine, mepivacaine, bupivacaine, and ropivacaine. Lidocaine and mepivacaine have a quick onset of effect, typically within 2 to 3 minutes after infiltration. Bupivacaine onset can take more than 10 minutes, and given its longer duration, can have more postoperative residual effects. Systemic toxicity is rare given the typically low volumes of medication (Table 1). [14] Agents such as lidocaine, bupivacaine, or tetracaine may come in eyedrop or gel formulations to allow for topical use. [15] [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Nondepolarizing Paralytics(Archived) -- Administration
retrieved_docs.content: A provider’s choice of non-depolarizing NMBD depends on the desired speed of onset, duration of action, route of elimination, and side effects. For instance, muscle relaxants with a rapid onset (i.e., rocuronium) and brief duration may be desirable when endotracheal intubation is the reason for paralysis. On the other hand, a longer-acting agent like pancuronium may also produce intubating conditions in 90 seconds, but at the cost of pronounced tachycardia within the patient and a block that may be irreversible by an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor (for over 60 minutes), increasing the risk of postoperative pulmonary complications. [12] The table below displays the characteristics of various non-depolarizing NMBDs. [10] [11] Duration of action for some of the most commonly used agents: Atracurium - 30 minutes or less Rocuronium - 45 to 70 minutes Pancuronium - 180 minutes or longer Vecuronium - 30 to 40 minutes

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2029
retrieved_docs.content: Local anesthetics are a pharmacologic class employed to achieve reversible loss of sensation in a specific region of the body. Within this class, mepivakain, prilokain, and propoxykain are identified as child agents of the broader local anesthetic category, while bupivakain and etidokain are also classified as local anesthetics. Recognizing that mepivakain, prilokain, propoxykain, bupivakain, and etidokain share the same fundamental mechanism of action enables clinicians to select appropriate agents for regional blocks, infiltration, or topical anesthesia and to appreciate their related pharmacologic properties, including variations in potency, duration of effect, and safety profile.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1416
retrieved_docs.content: General anesthetics constitute a diverse class of pharmacologic agents used to induce a reversible loss of consciousness, analgesia, amnesia, and muscle relaxation for surgical procedures. This class includes chloralosa, chloroform, enfluran, etomidát, fencyklidin, flurothyl, isofluran, ketamin, methohexital, oxid dusný, and propofol. Each of these agents functions as a “child” of the broader category of general anesthetics, reflecting their role as specific drugs that share the common purpose of producing and maintaining general anesthesia. While all serve the same overarching clinical goal, they differ in chemical structure, mechanism of action, potency, onset and duration of effect, and side‑effect profile, allowing clinicians to tailor anesthetic plans to the individual patient’s physiological status, surgical requirements, and risk factors. Understanding the full spectrum of these agents enhances patient safety, optimizes peri‑operative management, and supports informed selection of the most appropriate anesthetic regimen.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1324
retrieved_docs.content: General anesthetics encompass a diverse group of agents that are used to induce a reversible loss of consciousness for surgical and diagnostic procedures. The class of general anesthetics includes chloroform, enfluran, fencyklidin, flurothyl, methohexital, methoxyfluran, oxid dusný (nitrous oxide), and propofol. Each of these agents has distinct pharmacologic characteristics, such as differences in onset speed, duration of action, potency, and side‑effect profile, which influence their selection for specific clinical scenarios. Understanding the full spectrum of general anesthetic agents—ranging from the historic inhalational agent chloroform to the widely used intravenous agent propofol—allows clinicians to tailor anesthetic plans to individual patient needs, procedural requirements, and safety considerations, thereby optimizing peri‑operative care and outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1638
retrieved_docs.content: General anesthetics constitute a diverse group of agents used to produce reversible loss of consciousness and analgesia for surgical procedures. The category “anesthetics, general” includes chloroform, enflurane, fencyclidine, flurothyl, isoflurane, methohexital, and propofol as child agents, each representing a distinct chemical class with specific pharmacodynamic and pharmacokinetic profiles. In addition, xenon—a noble gas—also falls under the classification of general anesthetics, indicating that it shares the core functional purpose of inducing general anesthesia despite its unique mechanism of action. Together, these agents illustrate the breadth of substances that can be employed to achieve controlled, reversible unconsciousness in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.content: acting” includes mivakurium‑chlorid and rokuronium‑bromid. The anti‑inflammatory drug mefenesin is linked to propylene glycol, indicating the excipient’s role in its formulation. Together, these relationships clarify how each entity fits within larger pharmacological classifications, mechanisms of action, and formulation considerations, supporting comprehensive understanding for effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 53
retrieved_docs.content: classifications, specific active‑ingredient combinations, dosing strengths, delivery methods, excipient roles, and critical drug‑interaction contraindications to optimize pain‑management regimens, maximize analgesic efficacy, and mitigate risks for individual patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2694
retrieved_docs.content: Anestetika lokální, or local anesthetics, are classified within the broader category of neuropharmacologic agents; as such, they act on the nervous system to reversibly block nerve conduction and produce targeted loss of sensation, making them essential tools for providing localized analgesia during medical procedures while preserving overall consciousness. Recognizing anestetika lokální as neuropharmacologic agents informs clinicians about their pharmacologic properties, guides appropriate agent selection, and aids in anticipating and managing potential side effects, thereby enhancing patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1555
retrieved_docs.content: Aprepitant, scopolamine (skopolamin) and trimethobenzamide hydrochloride are antiemetic agents that may prevent الغَثَيان (nausea). Each of these medications is employed in clinical practice to reduce or avert nausea, contributing to improved patient comfort and outcomes in settings where nausea is a concern.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1477
retrieved_docs.content: General anesthetics constitute a broad class of agents used to induce reversible loss of consciousness, analgesia, amnesia, and muscle relaxation for surgical procedures. Within this class, chloroform, enfluran (enflurane), fencyklidin, flurothyl, isofluran (isoflurane), methohexital, oxid dusný, and propofol are recognized as specific child agents that share the overarching function of producing general anesthesia. Xenon is also classified as a type of general anesthetic, reflecting its role as an inhalational agent with anesthetic properties. Although these agents differ in chemical structure, potency, onset and recovery times, and side‑effect profiles, they are all integrated into the therapeutic repertoire for achieving controlled, reversible unconsciousness in patients undergoing operative or diagnostic interventions. Understanding the relationships among these individual anesthetic agents and the general anesthetic category supports informed selection, dosing, and monitoring strategies to optimize patient safety and perioperative care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1291
retrieved_docs.content: OPHTHALMOLOGICALS are a broad class of ophthalmic drugs that encompass several therapeutic subcategories, including ophthalmological anti‑inflammatory agents, ophthalmological antibacterial agents, and ophthalmological anesthetic agents. This class is also considered a subset of the combined category “ANTIINFLAMMATORY AGENTS AND ANTIINFECTIVES IN COMBINATION,” indicating that many ophthalmic preparations simultaneously provide anti‑inflammatory and anti‑infective effects. Within OPHTHALMOLOGICALS, “Local anesthetics, ophthalmologic” represent a specific type of ophthalmic anesthetic agent, highlighting the role of localized anesthesia in eye care. Together, these relationships illustrate how ophthalmic medications are organized by their primary actions—reducing inflammation, combating infection, and providing anesthesia—while also recognizing that combination products can address multiple therapeutic goals in a single formulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 56
retrieved_docs.content: antirheumatic agents, multi‑route formulations, enzyme inhibition, quinoline‑based vasodilation, prostaglandin antagonism, fatty‑acid chemistry, and distinct therapeutic strategies such as vascular modulation, topical ENT anesthesia, and combined analgesia, thereby supporting precise prescribing, pharmacologic research, and patient‑care decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2243
retrieved_docs.content: General anesthetics comprise a diverse group of agents that are classified under the category “anesthetics, general.” This group includes enfluran, fencyklidin, flurothyl, isofluran, ketamin, methoxyfluran, oxid dusný, and propofol. All of these compounds serve the primary purpose of inducing a reversible state of unconsciousness, analgesia, and muscle relaxation for surgical and diagnostic procedures. Although each agent possesses distinct pharmacologic properties—such as differences in onset, duration, metabolism, and side‑effect profile—they share the common function of providing general anesthesia. Recognizing the full spectrum of agents within the general anesthetic class aids clinicians in selecting the most appropriate drug for a given patient, optimizing perioperative management, and enhancing overall patient safety.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2081
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac complications that arise from anesthesia or other sedation administered during labor and delivery, especially when accompanied by a postpartum complication, are categorized as “Other and unspecified complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother.” This broader category is further classified under the concept of “التأثير المتأخر لمضاعفات الحمل والولادة والنفاس,” which refers to the delayed effects of pregnancy, childbirth, and postpartum complications. Consequently, such cardiac events are understood as part of the spectrum of maternal complications that can influence postpartum care and require specific attention in the management of the mother after delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1357
retrieved_docs.content: Fenazon and kokain are both classified as members of the broader category of analgesics and anesthetics, indicating that each of these agents is used for pain relief and for inducing anesthesia. As part of this pharmacologic class, they share the common purpose of managing pain and facilitating surgical or procedural sedation, and their inclusion in the analgesics and anesthetics group underscores their relevance to therapeutic strategies aimed at controlling nociception and providing anesthetic effects.

retrieved_docs.content: identified as an opioid analgesic, highlighting the therapeutic potential of kappa‑receptor targeting agents, which may offer analgesia with a different side‑effect profile compared with traditional mu‑agonists. Collectively, these entities demonstrate that opioid analgesics encompass a broad spectrum of dosage forms (injectable, oral solution, capsule), combination products, and both classic and investigational opioid molecules, all aimed at providing effective pain control across various clinical contexts.
